items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What weather does an anticyclone (high pressure system) bring?
items.correct: Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation — so anticyclones bring clear, settled weather: hot in summer, cold and frosty in winter.
items.misconception: High pressure brings stormy weather because the air pushes down hard.
items.random_wrong: Anticyclones bring heavy rainfall because they trap moisture.
items.why: Students conflate 'high pressure' with 'strong weather'. In fact, sinking air evaporates clouds — depressions (low pressure with rising air) bring storms, not anticyclones.

items.correct: The zigzag movement of sediment along a coast caused by waves approaching at an angle (swash up the beach) and gravity returning water perpendicular (backwash).
items.misconception: Sand moves along the beach because tides pull it sideways.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift is the rising of sea level over time.
items.why: Students confuse tides with waves. Longshore drift is a wave-driven process — the angle of swash vs straight-down backwash creates the zigzag, not tidal pull.

items.q: Which rock type is FORMED FROM COOLED MAGMA?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Igneous. “Sedimentary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where in the UK are uplands generally found?
items.why: It's North and west. “South-east” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how long is the UK's longest river, the Severn (km)?
items.why: The real figure is 354. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
